text: Alright, so here was a fun case I did recently. Sigmoid resection, diverticular disease. Lady had had kind of numerous bouts of diverticulitis in the past, but what she developed was a very tight sigmoid stricture. And she had had failed colonoscopies a couple of times. But luckily her endoscopist was able to get all the way around on her last try because we knew that we were planning on doing a resection and hoped to clear the rest of her colon, which we did. Just really impressive amount of scarring. And she has this like perfect omega loop of her sigmoid colon kind of crammed down in her pelvis. We're able to do this case with four eight millimeter robotic ports. No assistant port on this case, although I will do that on many of these cases. Prior hysterectomy, so some of the scarring could be due to that.

The surgeon disconnects the sigmoid colon from the left pelvis, revealing a tight loop of sigmoid colon that appears to be fistulizing with the rectum. The proximal sigmoid colon is freed from surrounding scar tissue, with the surgeon focusing on restoring normal anatomy and preparing to take the mesentery.
We're getting there. Ah, and finally...
The surgeon is working to straighten the rectum and break through the peritoneum in the pelvis to mobilize the rectum. This is necessary for specimen extraction and to facilitate the anastomosis.

The surgeon selects the distal margin for resection, where the sigmoid mesentery is visibly folded over on the medial aspect. The mesentery is freed up using a vessel sealer, which efficiently thins out the mesentery despite its thickness and scarring.

The surgeon has freed up the bowel and is ensuring there is no residual disease in the rectum. A sizer and rigid proctoscope are passed to confirm the area is clear. The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect the bowel, performing a half cycle burn. Excess fat is cleaned off to facilitate the upcoming purse string suture, which will be placed in the rectum.

The surgeon observes the healthy and well-perfused proximal colon. Due to significant scarring and disease, the specimen cannot be removed intact and requires chopping. The mesentery is separated from the colon to facilitate specimen extraction.

The surgeon positions the 29mm EEA anvil at two fixated points on the proximal colon, sliding the edge of the anvil into place. A true purse string suture is applied around the anvil using a canal stitch technique, with the tip-up grasper holding the anvil securely in the grooves. The mesenteric fat around the anvil is cleaned off using robotic assistance. The surgeon cinches the suture tightly, preparing to deploy the stapler spike through the center.

So it is just me here. I go down below and place a stapler, deploy the spike, run over to the robot while somebody just holds it in place, marry the two together, and then go back down below again. It's slightly cumbersome, I guess, but gosh, it's a whole lot faster than having to scrub back in and externalize and do the purse string suture that way. Do our leak test here. Rigid proctoscope up to and above the anastomosis. And things looked pretty darn good. So far this woman has done very well. She did go home the morning of post-operative day one. Thank you so much for watching.
